critic_raw:
The methodology is generally well-designed, with sensible temporal splitting, an appropriate baseline, and careful leakage controls. I found a small number of genuine issues worth raising, and one significant structural flaw.

**Issue 1: The 24-hour contact-intensity score is almost certainly a leaky feature.**

The methodology states this score 'summarizes support touches and routing events associated with the customer' and is justified because 'urgent cases often present as clustered interactions rather than isolated tickets.' The claim is that this score is 'already produced by the support platform and is included because [it reflects real-time signal].' The methodological assumption being made is that the version of this score available at *ticket-open time* is the same as the version recorded in the historical database.

Why this is likely wrong: production event-driven scores are frequently recomputed retrospectively when new events are logged. The database record for a ticket opened at 09:00 may reflect the contact-intensity score as it stood at 17:00 after the full escalation chain completed. If escalated tickets systematically generate more same-day contacts (follow-up calls, agent handoffs), the stored score will encode future information — specifically, information causally downstream of the urgency outcome itself. This is not a distributional assumption that can be verified by looking at train/val/test splits; it requires inspecting the ETL provenance of that specific feature.

Evidence one way or the other: examine whether the contact-intensity score for a given ticket is computed at ticket-creation time and frozen, or whether it is updated as subsequent events occur within the same 24-hour window. The smoking gun is a positive correlation between the score and *same-day post-opening* contact events. If the score is point-in-time frozen at ticket open, the concern dissolves. If it is a trailing aggregate that includes events after ticket open, it is a direct label proxy.

**Issue 2: The inter-rater filtering procedure conflates label noise with genuine labeling ambiguity, and the 80% agreement threshold is applied globally rather than class-conditionally.**

The claim is that tickets with inter-rater agreement below 80% are 'adversarially noisy' and should be excluded. The implicit assumption is that low agreement indicates corrupted ground truth rather than structurally ambiguous cases.

Why this is wrong: the cases where agent-assigned labels and external annotator labels disagree at high rates are precisely the *borderline urgent* cases — tickets that are ambiguous at the time of opening and for which urgency depends on information only available later (e.g., whether the customer's flight was eventually cancelled). Excluding these cases biases the training distribution toward easy, unambiguous examples. The model trained on filtered data will then face exactly these ambiguous cases in production and will be evaluated on them during test (since the 500 re-labeled samples are presumably a validation of training data quality, not a systematic scrubbing of test data). This creates a train-test distribution mismatch where the training set is cleaner than the test set in a way that inflates apparent performance. More concretely: if the urgent class is defined partly by ambiguous real-time signals, a model that never sees ambiguous positives during training will systematically misclassify them.

Evidence: compare the class distribution and feature distributions of excluded vs. retained tickets. If excluded tickets are disproportionately from the borderline region of the score distribution (i.e., predicted probability 0.4–0.6 by any reasonable model), this confirms the filtering is removing the hard cases. Additionally, measure test-set performance separately on tickets that would have failed the 80% agreement threshold (if such labels were available for the test period) versus those that would have passed.

**Issue 3: The baseline is misconfigured in a way that makes the comparison uninterpretable for the stated hypothesis.**

The stated goal is to isolate 'the effect of history.' The baseline uses 'the current [CLS] token and temporal features' with logistic regression. The proposed model uses a transformer joint encoder with a 2-layer MLP.

The problem: the proposed model differs from the baseline in at least three dimensions simultaneously — (a) model class (MLP vs. logistic regression), (b) input features (history included vs. excluded), and (c) optimization procedure (fine-tuning the shared encoder end-to-end vs. using frozen DistilBERT representations). The methodology claims 'any improvement is attributable to joint encoding, not model class complexity,' but this is only true if the DistilBERT encoder is frozen in both cases and the MLP/LR heads are the only architectural difference. If the proposed model fine-tunes DistilBERT while the baseline uses a frozen encoder passed to logistic regression, then a performance difference reflects both the richer architecture and the historical features simultaneously.

This is not a minor implementation detail — it is the core comparison the experiment is designed to support. Without a third condition (MLP on current message only, with the same fine-tuning setup), the attribution claim cannot be verified.

Evidence: the experimental protocol should include a current-only MLP with fine-tuned DistilBERT as a second baseline. If proposed model vs. this second baseline still shows improvement, the attribution to history is supportable. If most of the gain comes from MLP vs. LR (or fine-tuning vs. frozen), the history hypothesis is not confirmed.

**Summary:**
Issue 1 is the most critical because it could make the entire model valid only on historical data where the score is recomputed, not in real-time deployment. Issue 3 is a structural flaw in the experimental design that prevents the hypothesis from being tested. Issue 2 is a real but more moderate concern about representativeness of the training set. The temporal split, leakage controls, and evaluation protocol are otherwise well-conceived.
all_issues_raised: The 24-hour contact-intensity score may encode post-ticket-open events (including same-day escalation activity), making it a leaky feature that proxies the label rather than predicts it.

Excluding tickets with inter-rater agreement below 80% removes structurally ambiguous borderline cases from training while those same cases appear in test, creating a systematic train-test mismatch that inflates measured performance.

The baseline (logistic regression on current message only) confounds model class complexity with the presence of historical features, making it impossible to attribute performance differences to history alone without an additional ablation.
The rolling 24-hour contact-intensity score may encode escalation routing events, creating direct feature-target leakage because escalation defines the label.
Inter-rater filtering at 80% agreement systematically removes ambiguous cases, creating an easier-than-real evaluation distribution and inflating all reported metrics.
The baseline withholds structured metadata features from the comparison model, preventing attribution of any improvement to joint historical encoding rather than metadata access.

The 24-hour contact-intensity score is operationally constructed from the same escalation and priority signals that define the label, creating a near-circular feature that may dominate the model and make the text-encoding contribution undetectable.

Excluding tickets with inter-rater agreement below 80% systematically removes the ambiguous hard cases from both training and evaluation, making the benchmark easier than production and hiding model failures on uncertain inputs.
The baseline omits all structured features, so observed improvements cannot be attributed to history-aware encoding versus simply adding operational metadata—critical ablations are missing.

Macro F1 threshold optimization on a seasonally imbalanced validation set may produce a frozen threshold that is suboptimal for the test window if positive-class prevalence shifts, undermining the primary metric's reliability.

issues_raised: The rolling 24-hour contact-intensity score may encode escalation routing events, creating direct feature-target leakage because escalation defines the label.
Inter-rater filtering at 80% agreement systematically removes ambiguous cases, creating an easier-than-real evaluation distribution and inflating all reported metrics.
The baseline withholds structured metadata features from the comparison model, preventing attribution of any improvement to joint historical encoding rather than metadata access.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
This is a well-structured methodology. The temporal split, single-pass test evaluation, preprocessing-fit-on-train-only discipline, and the thoughtful baseline design are all correct. The fairness stratification and calibration evaluation are appropriate additions. I looked hard for fundamental flaws and found a small number of genuine issues worth naming.

**Issue 1: The rolling 24-hour contact-intensity score is a latent leakage vector**

The methodology states this score 'summarizes support touches and routing events associated with the customer' and is 'already produced by the support platform.' The claim is that it represents information available at prediction time. That is true in deployment, but the historical version of this score in the training set was produced by the production system *at the time of that ticket*. The question is whether the score's construction references events that are downstream of the urgency decision — for instance, if escalation events themselves are counted as 'routing events,' the score at the moment of labeling will reflect the escalation that defines the label. This would be a direct feature-target leakage: the score partially encodes whether the ticket was escalated, which is the label. The methodology does not audit the score's definition or exclude escalation-related events from its construction. Evidence either way: inspect the score's feature definition in the support platform. If escalation routing is one of the 'routing events' counted, the model will learn a near-tautological signal for part of the training set and will underperform in deployment where the score has not yet accumulated that signal.

**Issue 2: The inter-rater filtering procedure has an undisclosed selection bias that could distort all metrics**

The claim is that excluding tickets with <80% inter-rater agreement removes adversarially noisy examples. The mechanism of failure: hard-to-label cases are not random with respect to urgency or ticket type. Genuinely ambiguous tickets — borderline urgency, unusual customer profiles, novel complaint types — are precisely the cases most likely to fall below the agreement threshold. Removing them creates a cleaner but easier classification problem where the two classes are more separable than they are in reality. The model and baseline are both evaluated on this filtered distribution. The test set (months 31–36) was presumably filtered by the same rule, which means evaluation metrics reflect performance on unambiguous cases only. In deployment, the model will encounter the ambiguous cases that were excluded. This is not a marginal concern: if 10–20% of tickets are filtered, the reported F1 and AUC-PR can substantially overstate deployed performance. Evidence: report metrics separately on the filtered-out subset (labels can be the original agent-assigned labels, with noted noise). If model performance drops materially on that subset, the filtering has inflated headline numbers.

**Issue 3: The baseline is misspecified as a fair comparison for the hypothesis**

The claim is that 'any improvement is attributable to joint encoding, not model class complexity' because both models use the same DistilBERT encoder. The mechanism of failure: the proposed model has access to structured features (loyalty tier, escalation history, contact-intensity score) AND historical text, while the baseline gets only the current message embedding and temporal features. This conflates two separate effects: (a) the value of historical text representation and (b) the value of structured metadata. If the proposed model outperforms the baseline, it is impossible to attribute the gain to the joint encoding architecture versus simply having access to structured metadata that the baseline was denied. The correct additional baseline is logistic regression or a shallow MLP trained on the current message embedding plus all structured features but no historical text. Without this, the comparison does not isolate what the methodology claims to isolate. Evidence: adding this intermediate baseline is the direct test. If the intermediate baseline captures most of the gain, the joint encoding of historical text is not responsible for the improvement.

**Summary:** Issue 1 is a latent leakage risk requiring an audit of the contact-intensity score's definition — if it encodes escalation events it is a direct label leak. Issue 2 is a systematic evaluation inflation from ambiguous-case exclusion that affects all reported metrics. Issue 3 is a baseline misspecification that prevents attribution of improvement to the stated mechanism. These are distinct root causes: Issue 1 is a data construction problem, Issue 2 is a filtering bias, Issue 3 is an experimental design gap. None of the three requires empirical resolution — they are identifiable from the design alone, though Issue 1 requires inspecting the score definition.

issues_raised: The 24-hour contact-intensity score is operationally constructed from the same escalation and priority signals that define the label, creating a near-circular feature that may dominate the model and make the text-encoding contribution undetectable.

Excluding tickets with inter-rater agreement below 80% systematically removes the ambiguous hard cases from both training and evaluation, making the benchmark easier than production and hiding model failures on uncertain inputs.
The baseline omits all structured features, so observed improvements cannot be attributed to history-aware encoding versus simply adding operational metadata—critical ablations are missing.

Macro F1 threshold optimization on a seasonally imbalanced validation set may produce a frozen threshold that is suboptimal for the test window if positive-class prevalence shifts, undermining the primary metric's reliability.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
This is a well-designed methodology with sound fundamentals. The temporal split, leakage controls, and evaluation framework are all principled. I'll focus on the genuine issues I found rather than manufacturing concerns.

**Issue 1: The 24-hour contact-intensity score is a near-perfect proxy for the label**

The methodology describes a 'rolling 24-hour account contact-intensity score summarizing support touches and routing events.' Urgent cases are operationally defined as those escalated within 2 hours or assigned priority ≥3. A customer who has had multiple support contacts in the past 24 hours is, by construction, more likely to satisfy this definition—either because those prior contacts themselves triggered escalation signals or because the contact clustering is what prompted an agent to assign high priority. This feature is not predicting urgency from content; it is recapitulating the labeling process itself, partially. The risk is that the model learns to classify on this proxy rather than on the semantic content of the interaction. The model could achieve high F1 by weighting this score heavily while the text encoding contributes minimally—which would be fine for the stated goal of 'urgency classification' but bad if the goal is to understand whether joint history-aware encoding provides semantic lift. The comparison against the baseline (logistic regression on current message only) would then be answering the wrong question: not 'does historical context help understand what the customer is saying' but 'does having escalation-proxying operational features help predict escalation-proxying labels.' Evidence for this: inspect the feature importance or gradient attribution of the structured features vs. text embeddings. If contact-intensity score dominates, the history-aware text encoding is not doing the work the framing implies.

**Issue 2: The inter-rater filtering mechanism conflates label quality with label difficulty**

The methodology excludes tickets where inter-rater agreement is below 80%. This is intended to remove noisy labels, but it systematically removes the genuinely ambiguous cases—which are precisely the hard cases the model needs to learn to handle. Cases where a trained external annotator disagrees with the original agent label are often the cases where urgency is contextually determined rather than lexically obvious. By excluding them, the training and test sets skew toward unambiguous positive and negative examples. The result is that both training and evaluation are easier than production, and the model is never penalized for failing on ambiguous cases. In deployment, these ambiguous cases arrive continuously. Evidence for or against: measure the proportion of borderline-scoring model outputs (scores near 0.5) and compare to the excluded proportion. If the model has very few uncertain predictions, this is a sign the hard cases have been trained and tested away. Additionally, reporting F1 only on the filtered set without any analysis of the excluded-ticket distribution is a gap in the evaluation.

**Issue 3: The baseline is not a fair test of the hypothesis the paper claims to test**

The proposed mechanism is 'history-aware joint encoding helps.' The baseline is logistic regression on the current message only—no historical text, no structured features, no contact-intensity score. This means the full model adds simultaneously: (a) historical text, (b) structured features including contact-intensity score, and (c) transformer-based joint encoding with cross-sequence attention. Any observed improvement in the proposed model could be attributed entirely to (b)—adding operational structured features—without any contribution from (a) or (c). The design does not isolate the contribution of history-aware encoding from the contribution of structured features. A proper ablation would include at minimum: (i) transformer model with current text + structured features, no historical text; (ii) logistic regression with current text + structured features; and (iii) the full proposed model. Without these, the comparison cannot attribute improvement to the joint encoding architecture versus simply having better input features. Evidence: if (i) achieves comparable F1 to the full model, historical text encoding is not contributing meaningfully.

**Issue 4: Threshold selection on validation set with macro F1 as primary metric is weakly justified given class imbalance**

The methodology applies class weights in the loss but then reports macro-averaged F1. Macro F1 averages the F1 of the minority and majority class equally, which means optimizing macro F1 on a class-imbalanced validation set is sensitive to the specific imbalance ratio in months 25–30 and may not generalize to months 31–36 if that ratio shifts seasonally (which is expected given documented seasonal peaks). The threshold frozen from validation is optimal for the validation-set imbalance, not for the test-set imbalance. The methodology already lists AUC-PR as a secondary metric, which is the more appropriate primary metric for imbalanced classification under distributional uncertainty. This is not a fatal flaw but the choice of macro F1 as the primary optimization target for threshold selection is not well-grounded. Evidence: report the class-positive rate by month across the test window. If it shifts by more than a few percentage points, the frozen threshold will be suboptimal and recall-at-90%-precision may diverge significantly from validation expectations.
